coredata.dc:title: Excitation wavelength as additional dimension in cross-reactive sensor arrays
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Senyang
Zhang, Jinhui
Liu, Xiaohu
Zhang, Yating
Zheng, Yongchang
Yuan, Jinying
Bao, Jie
coredata.dc:description: Cross-reactive sensor arrays have powerful abilities in distinguishing multiple analytes. The larger the effective data volume is, the better the detection performance is. However, current collected data volume depends heavily on the amount of sensing materials involved. It is data-inefficient for each material and causes heavy workload to prepare materials. Herein, by introducing the dimension of excitation wavelength, we report a cross-reactive sensor array by using only one fluorescent material (β-cyclodextrin-modified quantum dots, QD-β-CD). The newly added dimension is demonstrated by collecting emission signals under four excitation wavelengths. Through machine learning algorithms, the cross-reactive sensor array can be used for the detection of single nitrophenol (NP) isomer (e.g. a superior detectability with a classification accuracy of 94.9 % for 0.01 μM p-nitrophenol), and concurrent quantitative analysis of complex binary/ternary NP mixtures in environment analysis. Our results indicate that the additional dimension of excitation wavelength can provide an effective way to increase the differences of multiple analytes in the design of cross-reactive sensor array. The idea of adding an extra dimension can be generally applicable for the fields of multidimensional information collection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cross-reactive sensor array
Excitation wavelength
Multidimensional information
Machine learning
Host-guest chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cross-reactive mechanism analysis for NP sensing
Detection of a mixture of two or more NPs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Beijing Innovation Center for Future Chips

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported in part by the Beijing National Research Center for Information Science and Technology under Grant BNR2019ZS01005 , and in part by the Beijing Innovation Center for Future Chips , Tsinghua University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A cross-reactive sensor array based on the dimension of excitation wavelength for NP detection.

Characterization of QD-β-CD. (a) Absorption spectrum and fluorescence emission spectrum of QD-β-CD. Inset: photos of QD-β-CD solution under daylight (left) and UV light (right). (b) FT-IR spectra of β-CD and QD-β-CD. (c) TEM images of QD-β-CD and the corresponding size distribution histogram.

Cross-reactive mechanism analysis for NP sensing. (a) Absorption spectra of three kinds of NP solution (50 μM, solid) and fluorescence excitation spectrum of QD-β-CD (dash). (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of QD-β-CD mixed with the same concentration (50 μM) of ONP, MNP, and PNP under different excitation wavelengths (325 nm, 350 nm, 375 nm, and 400 nm), respectively. (c) Fluorescence lifetime plot of QD-β-CD under the presence and absence of PNP (50 μM). (d) Relative fluorescence variation of QD-α-CD, QD-β-CD, and QD-APBA mixed with different NP of 50 μM under 350 nm excitation. Standard deviation was derived from at least three independent measurements.
3D image of data visualization after PCA process (The arrows indicate the direction of concentration increasing).

LS-SVR results of quantitative detection. (a-c) Comparison of predicted concentration and true concentration of a single NP solution. (a) ONP, (b) MNP, (c) PNP. (d-f) Comparison of true and predicted proportion of binary NP mixtures (Total NP concentration: 50 μM). (d) ONP + MNP, (e) MNP + PNP, (f) PNP + ONP. (Hollow star mark: trained concentration or proportion; solid star mark: untrained concentration or proportion).
The average accuracies of SVM classification for single NP detection.
The representative result of the test set of ternary NP mixtures by BP-ANN algorithm.

head.ce:title: Excitation wavelength as additional dimension in cross-reactive sensor arrays

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electronic Engineering, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electronic Engineering, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Organic Optoelectronics & Molecular Engineering, Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Organic Optoelectronics & Molecular Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Organic Optoelectronics & Molecular Engineering, Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Liver Surgery, Peking Union Medical College Hospital, Chinese Academy of Medical Science and Peking Union Medical College, Beijing, 100730, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chinese Academy of Medical Science and Peking Union Medical College

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Liver Surgery, Peking Union Medical College Hospital, Chinese Academy of Medical Science and Peking Union Medical College, Beijing 100730, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cross-reactive sensor arrays have powerful abilities in distinguishing multiple analytes. The larger the effective data volume is, the better the detection performance is. However, current collected data volume depends heavily on the amount of sensing materials involved. It is data-inefficient for each material and causes heavy workload to prepare materials. Herein, by introducing the dimension of excitation wavelength, we report a cross-reactive sensor array by using only one fluorescent material (β-cyclodextrin-modified quantum dots, QD-β-CD). The newly added dimension is demonstrated by collecting emission signals under four excitation wavelengths. Through machine learning algorithms, the cross-reactive sensor array can be used for the detection of single nitrophenol (NP) isomer (e.g. a superior detectability with a classification accuracy of 94.9 % for 0.01 μM p-nitrophenol), and concurrent quantitative analysis of complex binary/ternary NP mixtures in environment analysis. Our results indicate that the additional dimension of excitation wavelength can provide an effective way to increase the differences of multiple analytes in the design of cross-reactive sensor array. The idea of adding an extra dimension can be generally applicable for the fields of multidimensional information collection.

ce:section.ce:para: The “superpower” of the cross-reactive sensor arrays is to distinguish far more analytes than the number of sensor units. When encountering different analytes, each sensor unit will display the cross-reactivity to produce a distinct response. Similar to the human olfactory system, entire responses are processed together to output the result of the analyte [1]. Recently, cross-reactive sensor arrays have been a crucial tool in collecting information from multiple (mixed) analytes, e.g., volatile organic compounds, explosives, nanoparticles, metal ions, biomolecules, and polychromatic light [2–10]. For improving the performance of cross-reactive sensor arrays, the key is to optimize the cross-reactivity to collect effective multidimensional data. Larger sensing data can provide better detection performance for more kinds of single analytes or more complex mixtures [11–13]. However, in most cases, one material only corresponds to an intensity value or low-dimension data. Owing to data-inefficient utilization for each material, the large data volume greatly depends on the number of integrated materials [14,15], which leads to a heavy and tedious workload when preparing such a system. And the sensing data resulting from the single dimension of material is insufficient to distinguish accurately among similar analytes [16]. Thus, it is vital to add an additional dimension beyond materials for sensing data.

Fluorescence detection is an ideal way to collect additional information that contains the dimension of emission wavelength and excitation wavelength [17–20]. By employing fluorescent emission responses at multiple wavelengths, which are usualy called ratiometric fluorescence methods, it can effectively avoid noise interference from the instrument, temperature and other factors for self-calibration [21,22]. It is essential for these fluorescent emission spectra to contain two or more peaks. While for those fluorescent materials with only one emission peak, the response information of different excitation wavelengths is more worthy to be utilized in the cross-reactive sensor arrays. The introduction of excitation wavelength is the same as adding a new dimension of cross-response information, which can reduce the workload for various material preparation simultaneously. It takes a large advantage to present the cross-reactivity effectively for different analytes. However, the role of dimension of excitation wavelength has not been fully demonstrated.

Herein, by introducing the dimension of excitation wavelength, a cross-reactive sensor array composed of only one fluorescent material is realized. As shown in Fig. 1 , β-cyclodextrin-modified quantum dots (QD-β-CD) and nitrophenol (NP) isomers are selected as the fluorescent indicator and analytes, respectively. The former with a host molecule modified has a satisfactory performance in the fluorescent cross-reactive sensing [4,23,24], and the latter, including three different NPs with similar structures, are listed as the toxic and hazardous in soil and in aquatic environments [25–27]. The cross-reactive sensing mechanisms are based on the inner filter effect (IFE) and electron transfer between the host-guest structure of QD-β-CD and NP. By utilizing four excitation wavelengths (325 nm, 350 nm, 400 nm, and 410 nm) and machine learning algorithm, the cross-reactive sensor array shows superb performance in detecting each kind of NP. Furthermore, the resulted high accuracy and sensitivity could be expanded to more complex NP mixtures, which can concurrently quantitatively analyze two or three unknown analytes in a single measurement. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first time that the excitation wavelength has been used as additional dimension to prepare a fluorescent cross-reactive sensor array composed of only one material for mixture detection in environment analysis.

Cadmium oxide, oleic acid, 1-octadecene, trioctylphosphine and zinc diethyldithiocarbamate was obtained from Alfa-Aesar (Beijing, China). Glutathione (GSH), 3-aminophenylboric acid (3-APBA), o-nitrophenol (ONP), m-nitrophenol (MNP) and p-nitrophenol (PNP) was purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Se powder, oleylamine, 1-(3-dimethylainopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydro-chloride (EDC·HCl), N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide sodium (sulfo-NHS) and the whole interferential aromatic compounds with binary substituents (o-cresol, m-cresol, p-cresol, o-nitrotoluene, m-nitrotoluene, p-nitrotoluene, catechol, p-chlorophenol) were supplied by Innochem (Beijing, China). The two cyclodextrins (CD) including α-cyclodextrin (α-CD), β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) were purchased from Meryer (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH = 7.4) was purchased from Leagene (Beijing, China). All chemicals were used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ) was from Millipore water purification system. The water-soluble quantum dot with core-shell structure (QD-GSH) was synthesized as described previously [28,29].

Absorption spectra were characterized by the Agilent Cary 8454 UV–vis spectrophotometer with the 10 mm light path. Fluorescence excitation spectra and emission spectra were measured on the Horiba Fluorolog®3 spectrofluorometer system. The slits of excitation and emission was set as 2 nm and 4 nm, respectively. Fluorescent lifetime tests were recorded by Horiba Fluorolog-3-ultrafast with 450 W xenon lamp, double mono in the excitation side and iHR320 (with PPD-850 and CCD as L-side) in the emission side. The images of transmission electron microscope (TEM) were obtained from the 120 kV JEOL JEM-2010 microscope and analyzed by ImageJ software. Fourier transforms infrared (FT-IR) spectra was carried out on the Perkin-Elmer Spectrum 100 FT-IR spectrometer. The zeta potential was measured using Malvern zetasizer Nano ZSE.

QD-β-CD was prepared according to previous literature [30,31]. Briefly, 1.0 nmol of QD-GSH solution was mixed with the solution containing 0.3 μmol EDC·HCl and 0.3 μmol sulfo-NHS to activate the carboxyl of QD-GSH. After stirring for 30 min, 1.25 μmol of 3-APBA solution was added and stirred for 3 h. Excess 3-APBA was removed by two successive precipitations with ethanol. Purified QD-APBA solids were dispersed in 10 mM PBS solution (pH = 10.4). Then, 1.0 μmol β-CD was added and stirred overnight to form QD-β-CD. After two successive precipitations to remove unreacted β-CD with acetonitrile and dimethylsulfoxide (v:v = 1:1), purified QD-β-CD solids were stored at 4 °C for further experiments.

In the experiments of NP sensing, three isomer solutions (solvent, PBS, pH = 7.4) with different concentrations (0.02∼400 μM) were mixed with QD-β-CD solution by equal volume. The optimized concentration of QD-β-CD represented by optical density (OD) was 0.01, and the total volume was set as 3 mL. After incubation for 30 min, fluorescence emission intensity at 594 nm was measured under the excitation wavelength of 325 nm, 350 nm, 400 nm, and 410 nm in sequence. The quenching experiments of each kind of NP concentration were repeated three times. Binary and ternary NP mixtures were tested under the similar way by setting the total concentration of 50 μM with the smallest concentration gradient of 5 μM.

All data were processed in the Matlab R2014a, and the used machine learning algorithms contained principal component analysis (PCA), support vector machines (SVM), least squares support vector regression (LS-SVR) and backpropagation artificial neural networks (BP-ANN). PCA is a technique for noise reduction by only remaining the first several principal components, thus widely used in data visualization. SVM is a discrimination technique to find the most optimized decision boundary to separate different classes, and LS-SVR is the extended algorithm of SVM based on the least square optimization for the regression problem. BP-ANN, a kind of artificial neural networks, is ground on the backpropagation of training error to optimize weights to get a better performance in result prediction, which is a data-driven algorithm for application. All algorithms were modified based on the open-source library or statistical packages in the Matlab R2014a. Detailed data process can be seen in Supplementary Informaiton.

QD-β-CD is composed of QD and β-CD through a covalent linking of 3-APBA (Fig. S1). QDs are important fluorescent sensing nanomaterials with the characteristic that their fluorescent colors are tunable by size control [32,33]. And β-CD is one of the commonly used host molecule for molecular recognition in host-guest chemistry [34–36]. Because β-CD can bind multiple hydrophobic guest molecules with appropriate size through the host-guest interaction, the cross-reactivity makes it a promising candidate to be used in cross-reactive sensor array. As shown in Fig. 2 a, QD-β-CD solution dispersed in PBS is clear orange under daylight, and it emits strong orange fluorescence (λ em = 594 nm) under UV light (365 nm). It displays high fluorescent emission intensity in the vicinity of pH = 7.4 (Fig. S2), and keeps stable fluorescence within two weeks (Fig. S3). The FT-IR spectrum of QD-β-CD (Fig. 2b) contains the complete characteristic peaks of β-CD. Owing to high solubility of β-CD in the mixed precipitant, there are no obvious decrease of the strong band (ν O-H and ν C-O) when adding the number of purification, which indicates the successful synthesis of QD-β-CD (Fig. S4). The TEM results demonstrate favorable dispersion with an average particle size 5.8 ± 0.6 nm (Fig. 2c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cross-reactive mechanism analysis for NP sensing
ce:section.ce:para: NPs, including three isomers (ONP, MNP and PNP), are all considered as important industrial chemicals. Though their structures and properties are similar, each isomer has different toxicity to the environment and human health, which makes it necessary to distinguish NP isomers in the field of environment analysis. Therefore, the first is to unravel the cross-reactive mechanism of NP sensing. Because only one kind of QD-β-CD is used as the fluorescent indicator, the analysis of the cross-reactive mechanism is focused on the case under the presence of NP isomers.

It is believed that there are two quenching mechanisms between QD-β-CD and NPs [24]. The first one is primary IFE that requires large overlap between the absorption spectrum of analytes and the excitation spectrum of fluorescent indicators [37]. In Fig. 3 a, compared with the excitation spectrum of QD-β-CD, the absorption spectra of three NP solutions with the same concentration show different overlap areas. At the same wavelength, ONP, MNP, and PNP have distinct absorption values, which makes different contributions to fluorescence quenching through IFE. And as the concentration of NP increases, the influence of IFE becomes stronger (Fig. S5). Therefore, the obtained fluorescence emission intensity under different excitation wavelengths are distinct for sensing each kind of NP (Fig. 3b). Based on these differences, it is feasible to use the dimension of excitation wavelength to obtain different cross-reactive responses.

In addition to IFE, the host-guest interaction also plays a pivotal role in fluorescence quenching. The cross-reactivity for NP sensing is provided by dynamic quenching that exists between QD-β-CD and NP through electron transfer [28]. Taking PNP as an example, the difference in fluorescence lifetime (τ F) for QDs before and after β-CD modification demonstrates the effect of QD-β-CD in capturing PNP, which accords with the previous report (Fig. 3c and Fig. S6) [24]. Based on the special host-guest structure, it is easy to use other similar host molecules with different sizes of cavities (e.g. α-CD) to link with QDs for exploring the cross-reactivity of NP sensing. It is known that the binding constant for β-CD with ONP, MNP and PNP is 37.8, 44.2 and 90.1 M−1, respectively. And α-CD is a stronger host molecule to bind with NP (binding constants: 152.4, 135.1 and 628.5 M−1 for ONP, MNP and PNP) [23,38]. As shown in Fig. 3d, (F 0-F)/F 0 increase with the enhancement of host-guest interaction (QD-α-CD > QD-β-CD) when mixing QD-CDs with NP. For QDs without CD modification as the negative control, it displays a much lower (F 0-F)/F 0 than the QD-CDs. Therefore, in the QD-CD system, both IFE and host-guest interaction have proven to provide cross-reactive responses to different NPs.

The detection performance of cross-reactive sensor arrays is highly dependent on the difference of various cross-reactive responses. Thus, with the introduction of the dimension of excitation wavelength, it is necessary to select reasonable excitation wavelengths to obtain better cross-reactive responses. Generally, the maximum excitation wavelength of fluorescent indicator (QD-β-CD, λ ex = 350 nm) is considered as the optimal excitation wavelength because of the highest signal-to-noise ratio [39]. Nevertheless, for each kind of NP, the optimized excitation wavelength should be the one that reaches the largest (F 0-F)/F 0 (Fig. S7). Considering the quenching mechanism of IFE, the optimal excitation wavelengths for ONP, MNP and PNP are 410 nm, 325 nm, and 400 nm, respectively. They correspond to the position of the first excitonic absorption peak for each kind of NP. Including the maximum excitation wavelength, total of four excitation wavelengths are selected for the following experiments. Under the same excitation wavelength, (F 0-F)/F 0 shows an upward trend as the concentration of QD-β-CD decreases, indicating a higher sensitivity for NP detection (Fig. S7). However, a lower concentration of QD-β-CD results in lower emission intensity, thereby sacrificing the signal-to-noise ratio. Considering all three kinds of NPs, the optimal concentration of QD-β-CD solution is determined to be 0.01 OD.

Under the optimized experimental conditions, the results of fluorescent emission intensity under four excitation wavelengths for a single NP detection are shown in Fig. S8. The analysis results based on one excitation wavelength for comparison can be seen in Fig. S9. For each kind of NP, with the increase of NP concentration, the changes in the maximum emission intensity (λ em = 594 nm) of QD-β-CD under four excitation wavelengths are different. This distinguishable cross-reactivity under different excitation wavelengths is the key basis to employ the dimension of excitation wavelength. Due to the four-wavelength excitation, the datum of each sample for single measurement is a 4x1 matrix. For the process of these multidimensional data, machine learning algorithms have become powerful tools [40,41]. As shown in Fig. 4 , the original multidimensional data with four classes (ONP, MNP, PNP, and Blank) are separated explicitly after PCA processing by extracting the first three principal components (the corresponding three-view drawings can be seen in Fig. S10), which is the important basis for the following SVM algorithm. With the increase of concentration, data of three NPs distribute along different given tracks without overlap. Among all the data with NP (the concentration of NP is from 5 μM to 200 μM) and without NP (blank sample), 33 samples of each class (4*33 = 132 samples) were randomly selected as the test set, and others were considered as the training set. Table 1 is the average accuracies of SVM classification by a 10-fold validation. When the minimum concentration of NP is 5 μM (5∼200 μM), the accuracies of qualitative classification for all four classes are almost close to 100 %, which shows the superb performance in NP classification. In order to further explore its detectability for NP, the data with lower concentrations (1, 0.5, 0.1, and 0.01 μM) were then collected and processed. When the minimum concentration of NP is lower than 1 μM, the classification performance of ONP and MNP is quickly compromised due to the noise interference that covers the tiny difference between samples. But the classification performance for PNP still remains at a high level with a 94.9 % accuracy of classification, even though the lowest concentration of PNP is 0.01 μM. It demonstrates the superb detectability for low concentration of PNP presumably because there is a more effective IFE between QD-β-CD and PNP.

Further investigation for the performance of concentration prediction was carried out by using the LS-SVR algorithm [42]. Another group of sample data containing single NP with specific concentration was collected under the same experimental conditions. Together with the previous data, all data with the same NP were divided randomly into the training set and the test set with a ratio of 7:3. The correlation coefficient (R p) and root mean square error (RMSE p) of the prediction set are two important indicators for evaluating the performance of predicted results, which are calculated by using the following equations:
(1)
R p
=
1 −

i = 1

R M S
E p
=

i = 1
n
(

y i
)
2
n − 1
where
y i
and
y ˆ
i
are the true value and the predicted value of the i-th sample in the sample set, and
y ¯
is the average of the true values of all samples in the sample set. The performance of LS-SVR prediction models with R p close to 1 and a low RMSE p indicates the high accuracy and reliability of predicted results. As shown in the upper row of Fig. 5 , the predicted concentrations (including the trained and the untrained) and the true concentrations of test samples are mainly located on the diagonal (y=x). In addition, all three models have a R p close to 1 and a low RMSE p. The average relative error of LS-SVR results for ONP, MNP and PNP is 4.74 %, 6.35 % and 4.57 %, respectively. Through machine learning algorithm, the prediction of concentration can be extended to the whole concentration without the limitation of linearity range.

For a cross-reactive sensor array, it is crucial to avoid producing false-positive signals. Other aromatic compounds with binary substituents were chosen as the interferences to test the selectivity of the cross-reactive sensor array based on the dimension of excitation wavelength. As shown in Fig. S11, when mixed with these interferential compounds, the fluorescence emission intensity of QD-β-CD hardly changes significantly even if the structures of the interferences are similar to NP. Furthermore, the use of four excitation wavelengths amplifies the differences between NP and other interferential compounds. It can solve the problem of indistinguishable relative fluorescence value (F/F 0) for different compounds under one excitation wavelength, thereby providing a superior selectivity for NP sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of a mixture of two or more NPs
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to single NP detection, cross-reactive sensor arrays also have great advantages in mixed analytes detection that usually requires a large data volume. The introduction of dimension of excitation wavelength is the same as adding a new dimension of cross-reactive information, which increases the size of sensing data in the form of multiplication. In order to show the practicability of multi-wavelength excitation for mixture detection, experiments of binary and ternary NP detection were carried out.

In the binary NP mixtures, different NP compositions result in unique fluorescent responses under four channels of excitation wavelength, showing the cross-reactivity (Fig. S12). For qualitative classification, similarly, the SVM algorithm was carried out with a 10-fold validation. An average recognition rate of 91.77 % for three kinds of binary NP mixtures (ONP + MNP, MNP + PNP, PNP + ONP) is shown in Table S1. The ratio of each NP in binary NP mixtures can be further calculated by the LS-SVR algorithm. As shown in the bottom of Fig. 5, no matter whether the proportions of binary NP samples are in the training set or not, the predicted proportions of binary NP samples are all close enough to the true proportions, with data primarily located on the diagonal. The average relative error of predicted results of ONP, MNP and PNP is 4.61 %, 3.69 % and 4.08 %, respectively. Three SVR prediction models with a high R p close to 1 and a low RMSE p demonstrate the accuracy and reliability of binary NP mixtures sensing.

For a more complex mixture system containing three types of NP, the BP-ANN algorithm was utilized to predict each kind of NP concentration among ternary NP mixtures simultaneously. In Table 2 , it presents the representative result of the test set of ternary NP mixtures by BP-ANN algorithm (The whole experimental results can be seen in Table S2). Among these, it shows different recognition accuracies for each kind of NP at different mixing conditions. The RMSE p of ONP, MNP, and PNP in the ternary NP mixtures experiments are 3.70, 2.52, and 1.33, respectively, which are similar to those in the single NP quantitative detection. The full results in Table S2 demonstrate excellent performance for detecting three kinds of NP with different concentrations among the ternary NP mixtures simultaneously, especially a higher accuracy for PNP detection. It is noted that, the prediction performance for the ternary NP mixtures of our cross-reactive sensor array can be further improved by designing the delicate architecture of ANN and increasing the size of the training set, which we will explore in our further work.

In summary, by introducing the dimension of excitation wavelength, we have successfully developed a cross-reactive sensor array consisting of only one single fluorescent material (QD-β-CD) for the environment analysis. Based on the cross-reactive mechanisms for NP sensing (IFE and electron transfer by host-guest interaction), four excitation wavelengths (325 nm, 350 nm, 400 nm, and 410 nm) are used to evaluate its feasibility. Through machine learning algorithms, it could not only present superior detectability with a classification accuracy of 94.9 % for 0.01 μM PNP and selectivity in the detection of a single NP, but also be used to analyze multiple analytes quantitatively and simultaneously in more complex binary and ternary NP mixtures. The excitation wavelength is taken into account in the design of the cross-reactive sensor array, which can amplify the difference among analytes while reducing costs without preparing multiple sensing materials. This design can also offer more opportunities for various fields of multidimensional information collection in the future.

Senyang Liu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing. Jinhui Zhang: Investigation, Writing. Xiaohu Liu: Investigation, Writing. Yating Zhang: Investigation. Yongchang Zheng: Investigation. Jinying Yuan: Conceptualization, Supervision. Jie Bao: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the Beijing National Research Center for Information Science and Technology under Grant BNR2019ZS01005, and in part by the Beijing Innovation Center for Future Chips, Tsinghua University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.130183.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The optoelectronic nose: colorimetric and fluorometric sensor arrays

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Li, J.R. Askim, K.S. Suslick, The optoelectronic nose: colorimetric and fluorometric sensor arrays, Chem. Rev. 119 (2019) 231–292.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multiarray nanopattern electronic nose (E‐nose) by high‐resolution top‐down nanolithography

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Kang, S. Cho, J. Ryu, J. Choi, H. Ahn, H. Joo, H. Jung, Multiarray nanopattern electronic nose (E‐nose) by high‐resolution top‐down nanolithography, Adv. Funct. Mater. 30 (2020) 2002486.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tuning pendant groups of polythiophene on carbon nanotubes for vapour classification

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.-C. Tu, H.K. Svm, A. Thilini, L.T.L. Wallace, S. Moochhala, U.H. Yildiz, Al. Palaniappan, B. Liedberg, Tuning pendant groups of polythiophene on carbon nanotubes for vapour classification, Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem. 247 (2017) 916–922.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multichannel detection and differentiation of explosives with a quantum dot array

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.J. Peveler, A. Roldan, N. Hollingsworth, M.J. Porter, I.P. Parkin, Multichannel detection and differentiation of explosives with a quantum dot array, ACS Nano. 10 (2016) 1139–1146.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Identification of nanoparticles with a colorimetric sensor array

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Mahmoudi, S.E. Lohse, C.J. Murphy, K.S. Suslick, Identification of nanoparticles with a colorimetric sensor array, ACS Sens. 1 (2016) 17–21.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bispyrene/surfactant-assembly-based fluorescent sensor array for discriminating lanthanide ions in aqueous solution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Wang, L. Ding, J. Fan, Z. Wang, Y. Fang, Bispyrene/surfactant-assembly-based fluorescent sensor array for discriminating lanthanide ions in aqueous solution, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces. 6 (2014) 16156–16165.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence sensor array based on amino acid derived carbon dots for pattern-based detection of toxic metal ions

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mr. Senyang Liu received his bachelor’s degree from the Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua University in 2018. Currently, he is a Ph.D. student in Department of Electronic Engineering, Tsinghua University. His research focuses on the spectral analysis for chemical sensing, and information encryption and storage.

Ms. Jinhui Zhang received her B.S. degree from the college of electronic science & engineering, Jilin University, Changchun, China, in 2015. She is currently pursuing the Ph.D. degree with the Department of Electronic Engineering, Tsinghua University, Beijing, China. Her research interests include signal processing, machine learning, and applications of filter-based miniature spectrometer

Dr. Xiaohu Liu received his PhD in School of Biological Sciences, Nanyang Technological University in 2015. After two years of industrial research training, he moved to Tsinghua University as a postdoctoral researcher in 2017. His research interests focused novel biosensor development based on nanomaterials and designed peptides.

Ms. Yating Zhang received her bachelor's degree from Central South University, Changsha, China, in 2016. Currently, she is a Ph.D. student in the Department of Electronic Engineering, Tsinghua University. Her research involves the development of systems and algorithms for hyper-spectral imaging techonology and its applications in the medical field.

Prof. Yongchang Zheng received his PhD in clinical medicine from the Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences & Peking Union Medical College and School of Medicine, Tsinghua University. His research areas include hepatobiliary tumor pathogenesis, hepatitis B virus infection, new tumor embolization agents, three-dimensional liver imaging and imaging theory.

Prof. Jinying Yuan received her B.Sc from the Department of Applied Chemistry, University of Science and Technology of China (USTC) in 1987, and received her Ph.D. in the Department of Polymer Science and Engineering, USTC in 2000. After two years of postdoctoral research at the College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Peking University, she joined the Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua University in 2002, where she became a full professor in 2011. Her research interests are focused on the methodology of polymer synthesis and functional polymer materials, especially on stimuli responsive materials and controlled radical polymerization.

Prof. Jie Bao received his B.S. degree in chemistry from Tsinghua University, Beijing, China, in 2006, and the Ph.D. degree in chemistry from Brown University, Providence, RI, USA, in 2010. He continued as a postdoc in the Department of Chemistry at Massachusetts Institute of Technology until 2013 when he joined the faculty of the Department of Electronic Engineering at Tsinghua University. His research interest includes semiconductor nanomaterials, optoelectronic devices, computational spectral devices and imaging, and their applications.
